title: “Justice Delayed; Is It Justice Denied?”
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy; the frozen source chapter remains unchanged and authoritative. The page images restore the OCR-omitted opening `உடன்பிறப்பே,` and confirm `7 ஆயிரத்திற்கும் மேற்பட்ட விசாரணைக் கைதிகள்`. A line of scan/OCR symbols preceding the Coimbatore case has been removed, while the source's genuine English legal phrases are preserved. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: On 5 September 2014, a Bench comprising Chief Justice of India R.M. Lodha and Justices Kurian Joseph and Rohinton Fali Nariman delivered a general ruling in an important case. The news appeared in every daily newspaper.

The Supreme Court directed district courts across the country to release an accused person in a criminal case if that person, while awaiting trial in prison, had already served half the maximum sentence prescribed for the alleged offence. The Court stated that the order would not apply to undertrial prisoners accused of offences punishable by death. Justice Lodha delivered this important ruling shortly before his retirement from the Supreme Court.

District courts must take steps to visit and inspect the prisons within their jurisdiction once a week. They must examine how long a prisoner would have been confined if convicted. If an undertrial has already spent more than half that period in prison, the person may be identified and released immediately under Section 436-A of the Code of Criminal Procedure. Two months have been allowed for this exercise, which district judges must begin on 1 October. Judges within each district, including Sessions Judges, must visit the prisons, conduct inspections and identify undertrial prisoners who have spent half of the possible sentence in custody. The Supreme Court has directed district judges to issue orders releasing those prisoners.

The Supreme Court Bench adjourned the case to 8 December. By then, the Registrars General of the High Courts in the States must determine how many undertrial prisoners the district courts have released under this ruling and send a report to the Registrar General of the Supreme Court.

Of the 3,81,000 people confined in prisons throughout India, 2,54,000 are undertrial prisoners. In other words, 66.6 per cent of the prison population consists of people awaiting trial. Tamil Nadu has nine central prisons, five special sub-jails, nine district prisons, ninety-five sub-jails and three special prisons for women. In Chennai’s Puzhal prison alone, there are 2,223 male and 120 female undertrial prisoners. Across Tamil Nadu, more than 7,000 undertrial prisoners are reportedly held in prisons.

Many of these undertrial prisoners have already remained in prison longer than the maximum sentence they could receive even if the charges against them were proved. Section 436-A of the Code of Criminal Procedure says that an undertrial may be released on a personal bond, or without surety, after spending half the maximum possible sentence for the alleged offence in prison.

In 2005, senior advocate Bhim Singh, head of the Kashmir Legal Aid Committee, an organisation engaged in legal struggles for prisoners’ rights, filed a public-interest petition in the Supreme Court. His petition stated that several Pakistani prisoners had been confined in prisons for more than ten years without trial. Hearing that petition, the Supreme Court had already directed in 2008 that cases concerning undertrial prisoners be heard expeditiously and concluded within one year. It is in that very case that the Supreme Court has now delivered the ruling I described above.

As a result, many people held in different prisons throughout India will receive an opportunity for release. Nearly three crore criminal cases are pending in India, but there are only 16,000 judges to hear them. Without a ruling of this kind, when would all these cases ever be completed? For how many more years would undertrial prisoners remain in jail? It is no exaggeration to say that this Supreme Court ruling offers an answer to those questions. I welcome the ruling and thank all the Justices responsible for it.
Once these undertrial prisoners are released, prison overcrowding will be reduced considerably. The burden on prison staff will become lighter, helping them administer the prisons efficiently.
On 7 September 2014, *The Hindu* published details of a case under the headline “A Classic Case of Judicial Delay.” Some Tamil newspapers also carried the report.

A civil case between Kalaivani and Parimalam on one side, and Balakrishnan and Pradeep Kumar on the other, had been heard by the Subordinate Court in Coimbatore. The Subordinate Court’s judgment was appealed before the Coimbatore District Court. But the records required to hear the appeal did not arrive at the District Court from the Subordinate Court.

On the ground that the court was “Awaiting Records,” the case was adjourned eighty-nine times. On only one or two occasions was “transfer of the judge” given as the reason. The same single reason for all the other adjournments was recorded in the District Court register. Consequently, the case was delayed for five years and four months. Kalaivani and Parimalam then petitioned the High Court for an order directing the case to be concluded quickly, and the matter came before Justice Vimala.

In her judgment, Justice Vimala said: “The Subordinate Court in which the suit was filed and the District Court hearing the appeal are located within the same complex. Even so, the case was adjourned eighty-nine times on the ground that the court was awaiting the records. This caused delay in the trial. The Coimbatore Subordinate Court, which heard the civil suit, must immediately send the case records to the District Court hearing the appeal. The District Court must conclude the case within one month.” She also proposed several further reforms to the judiciary in her judgment.

This case is a fine example of how greatly the courts themselves can delay proceedings. Writing about the High Court Judge’s ruling, *The Hindu* observed: “She asked whether the court was justified in not rendering timely justice and waiting for records for years together in an era of information and communication technology.” That is a telling explanation of the extent to which some cases are delayed in our courts.

In another ruling delivered a few weeks ago, the Supreme Court advised the Prime Minister and every State Chief Minister not to appoint persons facing criminal charges as Ministers. Naturally, the question also arose: what if a Chief Minister is themselves facing criminal charges?

The Supreme Court further directed the Union Government to take steps to expedite cases against Members of Parliament and Members of Legislative Assemblies and conclude them within one year of charges being framed. Following this, the Union Law Ministry held consultations in Delhi last week. Union Law Minister Ravi Shankar Prasad then wrote to every State Chief Minister and Chief Justice, asking them to take the necessary steps to expedite and conclude cases against MPs and MLAs, and to monitor proceedings in the lower courts.

Yet it was this same Union Law Minister who, only a few days ago, met and spoke with Chief Minister Jayalalithaa at the Secretariat in Chennai at a time when the disproportionate-assets case against her, after continuing for eighteen years, was about to reach judgment. Newspapers reported that meeting. It is also worth noting that he had earlier appeared as counsel for Jayalalithaa in that case.

Even so, when a reporter asked how I viewed the meeting between the Chief Minister and the Union Law Minister, I replied that it would be neither proper nor fair to blame the Union Government on that account. I said I trusted that a Law Minister serving under Modi would conduct himself in a manner that brought no discredit upon the Union Government.

At a time when some judges are making great efforts both to address cases delayed for years on ordinary grounds and to end the suffering of people who languish in prison for years in the name of an inquiry, consider what happens in another case. The Supreme Court orders that the trial be completed within four months. Yet it is not completed within that period. Some reason is offered, and another three months are sought and obtained from the Supreme Court. The trial is not completed even within those three months, and another two months are requested. If the accused had been ordinary people, would such accommodation and repeated adjournments have been available to them? That is the question!

Under the Supreme Court’s present ruling, approximately 2,500 people across Tamil Nadu have been released in a single day. Without permitting discrimination, and opening a path to freedom for undertrial prisoners generally, this ruling shows a deep understanding of the maxim, “Justice delayed is justice denied.” On behalf of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam, I welcome it.
With affection,
M.K.
9 September 2014
